title: Lipochaeta integrifolia
intro: Lipochaeta integrifolia, also known as Lipochaeta or Kāmaʻō, is a perennial herb native to Hawaii, characterized by its shrub-like growth and distinctive white or yellow flowers. The plant is valued for its potential health benefits, including anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which may aid in the treatment of various conditions such as skin irritations, wounds, and respiratory issues. Bioactive constituents of Lipochaeta integrifolia include sesquiterpene lactones, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. Traditionally, Lipochaeta integrifolia is used in herbal preparations such as teas, infusions, and salves, which are often used to promote wound healing, reduce inflammation, and soothe skin irritations.
taxonomy: Lipochaeta integrifolia is classified within the Asteraceae family, which is one of the largest and most diverse families of flowering plants. This family is commonly known as the daisy or sunflower family. Lipochaeta integrifolia is a species of flowering plant native to Hawaii, and its classification is further divided into the subfamily Asteroideae. The Asteroideae subfamily consists of various tribes and genera, with Lipochaeta integrifolia belonging to the tribe Heliantheae. Overall, the classification of Lipochaeta integrifolia is a key part of understanding its evolutionary history and relationships with other plant species.
